summary: This paper addresses the learning of solution operators for physical field equations (PDEs) posed on geometric meshes and manifolds, approached from a function-space viewpoint. The central observation is that the Hodge decomposition of the space of discrete differential forms provides an orthogonal splitting of the solution operator that isolates topology-dominated degrees of freedom from geometry-driven local dynamics, thereby avoiding the spectral interference a single learned operator would suffer. Concretely, the authors use the spectrum of the Hodge Laplacian to separate the signal into a low-frequency component that captures global, topological and coarse-geometric structure (represented via discrete differential forms and Hodge Laplacian eigenbases) and a high-frequency component that captures local dynamics, which is learned by lifting the input into an ambient Euclidean space, applying a neural operator there, and pulling the result back onto the mesh. Orthogonal projection with respect to the Hodge inner product keeps the two branches separate, and a commutator-style correction term accounts for the non-commutativity introduced when the two are fused. The resulting Hybrid Eulerian-Lagrangian architecture encodes an algebraic inductive bias the authors term Hodge Spectral Duality (HSD). Across three tasks, including settings with non-trivial topology, the method is reported to improve accuracy and efficiency over a range of neural-operator baselines while better respecting physical invariants, and a complexity analysis argues for favorable scaling once the eigenbases are precomputed.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The core idea is elegant and, to my knowledge, genuinely novel: using the Hodge spectrum to separate low- and high-frequency behavior and assigning a distinct, structurally appropriate operator to each is a principled application of a well-understood body of mathematics (Hodge theory, discrete differential forms, simplicial complexes) to neural operator learning. The construction is mathematically well grounded, the motivation for the dual-branch design is clearly articulated at the level of intuition, and the paper backs it with a sensible analysis of spectral bias and computational complexity, including the observation that, once the Hodge Laplacian eigenbases are available, the method enjoys better theoretical complexity than the baselines. On significance, the approach is attractively unifying: it targets PDEs on arbitrary Riemannian manifolds and higher-order simplicial complexes, aims for a solution operator largely independent of the particular discretization, and supports this with experiments. The empirical evaluation is careful and broad, spanning several tasks and baselines and including a topologically non-trivial manifold, and the reported gains in accuracy, efficiency, and fidelity to physical invariants are substantial. Implementation details such as parameters, layer configurations, and learning curves are reported.

The main weakness is accessibility and presentation. The paper leans heavily on specialized background and at several points assumes the reader is already fluent in it: for instance, it invokes a Hodge Spectral Duality result without a self-contained statement, and the connection between the topological invariants and the vector fields being learned is not made obvious. The most technical material suffers most. The operators A^k in Section 4 are referenced but never explicitly specified, the corresponding decomposition is not fully written out even in the appendix, and the high-level engineering of Appendices E and F, comprising the ambient lift and pullback, Whitney reconstruction, kernel density estimation, and how these steps are chained together, is difficult to follow for anyone outside the immediate subfield. A dedicated exposition of the key theorem and of the topology-geometry dichotomy, explicitly tying the governing equations together, would materially improve the paper.

There are also several points where the formalism is imprecise or under-general. The background presents the Hodge star as a map from k-cochains to k-cochains, which does not match the standard definition mapping k-forms to (n-k)-forms, and this should be reconciled. It is unclear whether the governing equation is intended to be time-dependent, whether generic boundary conditions are supported, and whether inputs beyond f_k enter the operator. More substantively, although the title foregrounds the Hodge decomposition, the low-frequency branch appears to store spectral coefficients ordered by eigenvalue rather than explicitly separating the harmonic, curl, and gradient parts; because the curl eigenvalues of the k-th Hodge Laplacian coincide with the gradient eigenvalues of the (k+1)-th, the construction risks duplicating information, and an ablation isolating these parts would clarify how much of the Hodge structure is actually exploited. On the empirical side, the most recent baseline is from 2021, so positioning against the last few years of neural-operator work is left open; it is also notable that the strongest baselines (FNO-3D, DeepONet) are not topology-aware in their standard form, which raises the question of whether the benchmarks are topologically demanding enough or whether the baselines are fully tuned. Finally, the eigenbasis computation restricts the method to relatively small simplicial complexes, and the architecture as presented reads as somewhat handcrafted per problem rather than a single unified pipeline; how each task's inputs and outputs map onto the formalism, how preserved modes such as harmonic components are initialized, and why particular design choices were made (the gMLP gating, Whitney plus KDE in the fiber branch) all deserve fuller justification.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. The background presents the Hodge star operator as a map from k-cochains to k-cochains, whereas the standard definition maps k-forms to (n-k)-forms. Could you clarify and reconcile this discrepancy?
2. The low-frequency branch appears to store spectral coefficients ordered by eigenvalue without explicitly separating the harmonic, curl, and gradient components, and the curl eigenvalues of the k-th Hodge Laplacian coincide with the gradient eigenvalues of the (k+1)-th. Is information effectively duplicated, and could you provide an ablation that isolates the harmonic part and, separately, the curl and gradient parts, using only the relevant eigenvectors in the decomposition?
3. Does the method require the geometry and topology of the manifold to be known in advance, or can it discover topological invariants from data? And does the spectral decomposition guarantee that all topological invariants are captured within the base (low-frequency) component?
4. The strongest baselines (FNO-3D, DeepONet) are not topology-sensitive in their standard implementations and the most recent baseline is from 2021. Does this reflect insufficient tuning of the baselines, benchmarks with limited topological complexity, or both, and how does the method compare against more recent neural operators? Please also report training and inference time relative to the baselines across all experiments.
5. Do the three experimental tasks fit the formalism of Eq. (1) and the architecture in a unified way, or does each require per-problem handcrafting? Please specify the inputs and outputs of each task, note any limitations for different PDE types, and indicate whether code will be released.
limitations: The limitations are largely acknowledged, but two points deserve more explicit treatment. First, the scalability constraint imposed by computing the Hodge Laplacian eigenbases, which effectively restricts the method to relatively small simplicial complexes, should be discussed directly. Second, the heavy reliance on specialized mathematical background and the incompletely specified operators and engineering steps limit accessibility, and a more self-contained exposition would help the reader gauge the method's scope and applicability.
